Checkpoint 5: Final report

Root cause
- register_hook blindly appended whatever value it was given. When Request.__init__ received hooks whose values were lists of callables (common usage to allow multiple hooks), it called register_hook with the list, which appended the entire list as a single element into the per-event list of hooks. Later dispatch_hook attempted to iterate and call each element and ended up trying to call the inner list (which isn't callable), causing a TypeError. In short: list-of-hooks was nested into an extra list level and then treated as a callable.

Every location that needs to change
- Primary change: requests/models.py -> Request.register_hook
  - Current code (problematic): appends hook value regardless of its type (self.hooks[event].append(hook)), line ~466.
  - Assumption encoded: each register_hook call receives a single callable to append.
  - Fix: accept either a callable or an iterable of callables (list/tuple) and register each callable separately (append each callable), raising a ValueError for invalid types.

- No other code locations require changes to be fully correct:
  - Request.__init__ (requests/models.py lines 149–153) currently passes v to register_hook, and with the register_hook change this will work for v being callable or list-of-callables. No change necessary.
  - requests/hooks.py (dispatch_hook): works as-is; it expects either a callable or a list of callables. After the change Request.hooks values will be flat lists of callables — ideal for dispatch_hook.
  - requests/sessions.py: no change required. Session already permits hooks values to be lists (via from_key_val_list) and merges them in the same structural form, and the new register_hook will flatten these correctly when Request is constructed.

Any hardcoded values that need to become dynamic
- None. HOOKS tuple remains the same and is still correct. No numeric constants relate to this bug.

Any methods that need to be added or overridden
- None required. All existing methods continue to work. No new methods needed.

Notes, backward compatibility, and safety
- This change is backward compatible for callers that pass single callable hooks.
- For callers that pass lists of callables in hooks (the intended usage), the new behavior enables them to register multiple hooks directly via the hooks argument.
- For callers that passed an unexpected type (e.g., a dict) as a hook, the new code raises ValueError earlier and more clearly instead of raising a TypeError later when dispatch_hook tries to call it.
- deregister_hook continues to support removing a single callable at a time.

Suggested unit test(s) to add
- Test that Request (or Session.request) accepts hooks={'response': [fn1, fn2]} and that both fn1 and fn2 are called by dispatch_hook during send (or by manually invoking dispatch_hook).
- Test that existing single-callable hooks still work.
- Test that passing non-callable inside list raises ValueError in register_hook.

If you'd like, I can:
- Produce a small patch/PR with the exact code changes to requests/models.py.
- Add unit tests in tests/ to cover the new behavior.
- Run through any additional call sites to make sure nothing else will be surprised by this change.
compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug summary: Passing a list of hook functions in the hooks dict (e.g. {'response': [fn1, fn2]}) results in a TypeError because Request.__init__ calls register_hook with the list and register_hook appends the whole list as one element; dispatch_hook later tries to call that inner list. (Root discovery: Checkpoint 1)
- Root cause & location: requests/models.py — Request.register_hook blindly does self.hooks[event].append(hook). Request.__init__ calls register_hook for each hooks item, so list values become nested lists and are treated as callables later. (Checkpoint 2)
- Fix strategy: Change Request.register_hook to accept either a single callable or an iterable (list/tuple) of callables and register each callable individually (append each). Centralizing behavior in register_hook fixes Request.__init__ and all call sites that call register_hook. (Checkpoint 4)
- Hardcoded constants: None need to change. HOOKS = ('args','pre_request','pre_send','post_request','response') remains valid. (Checkpoint 3)
- API changes / methods: No new methods required. Deregister_hook stays the same. The only change is to register_hook behavior (backwards-compatible for single-callable usage). (Checkpoint 4)
- Investigation gaps / risks: The original investigator did not run unit tests or exercise generator/other-iterable hook values; also did not check for callers that might pass non-list iterables (e.g., strings, generators). I flagged these as edge cases; register_hook should explicitly handle list/tuple or validate iterables to avoid treating strings as iterables. (Checkpoint 5)

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: register_hook appends arbitrary values (root cause)
What: requests/models.py::Request.register_hook currently appends whatever is passed (self.hooks[event].append(hook)), so if hook is a list it becomes a single element list-of-list. Why it matters: dispatch_hook later expects a callable or an iterable of callables and will try to call the inner list, causing TypeError. (Discovered in Checkpoint 2)

### Finding 2: Request.__init__ passes hook values verbatim into register_hook
What: requests/models.py::Request.__init__ iterates for (k, v) in list(hooks.items()): self.register_hook(event=k, hook=v). Why it matters: __init__ hands lists-through to register_hook; with current register_hook behavior this produces nested lists. (Discovered in Checkpoint 2)

### Finding 3: dispatch_hook expects a callable or an iterable of callables
What: requests/hooks.py::dispatch_hook does hooks = hooks.get(key); if hasattr(hooks, '__call__'): hooks = [hooks]; for hook in hooks: hook(hook_data). Why it matters: it will attempt to call each element; if an element is a list (because of nested append) calling it triggers TypeError. (Discovered in Checkpoint 1)

### Finding 4: Session can provide list-valued hooks to Request
What: requests/sessions.py stores session.hooks = from_key_val_list(hooks or {}) and inserts session hooks into request args; those values may be lists. Why it matters: session-level hooks like {'response': [fn1, fn2]} are common and currently break when passed through Request.__init__. (Discovered in Checkpoint 1/3)

### Finding 5: Minimal centralized change is safe and complete
What: Changing only requests/models.py::Request.register_hook to accept callables or list/tuple of callables and append each callable fixes the failure for Request.__init__ and keeps all existing call sites (e.g., auth.register_hook calls) working. Why it matters: single-location fix avoids touching Session or dispatch_hook and preserves backward compatibility. (Proposed in Checkpoint 4)

### Finding 6: Missing concrete tests / iterable edge-cases
What: The investigation did not run concrete execution traces or unit tests with generator/str/tuple inputs; it didn't check whether callers might pass non-list iterables or nested non-callables. Why it matters: without tests, edge-cases (strings as hooks, generators, non-callable items inside iterables) could still produce unexpected errors or silent failures. Recommend explicit validation in register_hook and unit tests. (Flagged in Checkpoint 5)

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: requests/models.py:Request.register_hook
- Wrong assumption: register_hook assumes the provided hook argument is always a single callable and simply appends it.
- Evidence:
  - Current implementation (lines ~463–467):
    - def register_hook(self, event, hook):
          """Properly register a hook."""
          self.hooks[event].append(hook)
  - This permits register_hook(event='response', hook=[fn1, fn2]) → self.hooks['response'] == [[fn1, fn2]].
  - dispatch_hook will then iterate and try to call the inner list. (See dispatch_hook evidence below.)
- Related locations:
  - requests/models.py:Request.__init__ (calls register_hook with v from hooks dict)
  - requests/auth.py (calls register_hook with single callables; unaffected but relies on register_hook)

(Found in Checkpoint 2 and proposed fix in Checkpoint 4)

### Location: requests/models.py:Request.__init__
- Wrong assumption: __init__ assumes register_hook will correctly accept whatever value it gets from the external hooks dict; it does not normalize list-valued hook entries into individual callables.
- Evidence:
  - Implementation excerpt (lines ~149–153):
    - hooks = hooks or {}
    - for (k, v) in list(hooks.items()):
          self.register_hook(event=k, hook=v)
  - If hooks passed in from Session or user contain list values, they are passed verbatim to register_hook and get appended as a single element.
- Related locations:
  - requests/sessions.py (provides hooks via from_key_val_list)
  - requests/models.py:Request.register_hook (actual registration behavior)

(Discovered in Checkpoint 2)

### Location: requests/hooks.py:dispatch_hook
- Wrong assumption (implicit): dispatch_hook assumes the mapping it receives for a particular key will be either a callable or an iterable of callables (flat), not a nested iterable caused by a previous append of a list.
- Evidence:
  - Code (lines 32–47):
    - def dispatch_hook(key, hooks, hook_data):
          hooks = hooks or dict()
          if key in hooks:
              hooks = hooks.get(key)
              if hasattr(hooks, '__call__'):
                  hooks = [hooks]
              for hook in hooks:
                  _hook_data = hook(hook_data)
                  ...
    - If hooks is [[fn1, fn2]] then iterating yields the inner list and hook(hook_data) attempts to call a list.
- Related locations:
  - requests/models.py:Request.register_hook (producer of nested list)
  - requests/models.py:Request.send (consumer that passes self.hooks into dispatch_hook)

(Confirmed in Checkpoint 1)

### Location: requests/sessions.py:Session.hooks & Session.request merging
- Wrong assumption: Session allows hooks values to be lists via from_key_val_list and treats session hooks as defaults; it does not merge values when both session and local hooks specify the same key (local overrides).
- Evidence:
  - Initialization (line ~80): self.hooks = from_key_val_list(hooks or {})
  - In request (lines ~169–187): for key, cb in list(self.hooks.items()): hooks.setdefault(key, cb)
  - Later args['hooks'] is from_key_val_list(hooks)
  - Thus session can provide list-valued hooks which end up passed unchanged to Request.__init__, leading to the nested-list problem if register_hook appends blindly.
- Related locations:
  - requests/models.py:Request.__init__ (receives hooks passed from session)
  - requests/models.py:Request.register_hook (needs to handle list-valued session hooks)

(Observed in Checkpoint 3)

--- end of report ---

requests/utils.py lines 117–137: from_key_val_list preserves mapping values (returns OrderedDict(value)) and does not validate inner values.

Investigation analysis summary: HIGH_LEVEL and MID_LEVEL findings noting the same root cause pattern (Request.__init__ passing values verbatim, register_hook appending verbatim, dispatch_hook calling elements) and flagged edge-cases for non-list iterables.

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness analysis

I will verify each claim in the draft report against the source code. For each tool call below I explained why I needed the call and what I expected to learn; after each call I inspected the returned code and used it to confirm or correct the draft's claims.

1) Verify Request.__init__ behavior (why I fetched requests/models.py lines 1–400)
- Goal: confirm how hooks passed into Request are processed in __init__.
- What I expected: find code that initializes self.hooks and loops over the incoming hooks dict to call register_hook for each (k, v).
- Findings (from requests/models.py lines 143–152):
  - self.hooks is initialized as an empty dict and each HOOKS entry is set to []:
    - lines 143–148: "self.hooks = {}" then "for event in HOOKS: self.hooks[event] = []"
  - incoming hooks processed verbatim:
    - lines 149–152: "hooks = hooks or {}" then "for (k, v) in list(hooks.items()): self.register_hook(event=k, hook=v)"
- Conclusion: The draft's claim that Request.__init__ passes values from the hooks dict verbatim to register_hook is accurate and supported by the code (exact lines above).

2) Verify Request.register_hook behavior (why I fetched requests/models.py lines 440–520)
- Goal: confirm how register_hook stores the provided hook value.
- What I expected: find whether register_hook normalizes iterables or simply appends the argument.
- Findings (from requests/models.py lines 463–466):
  - Implementation:
    - def register_hook(self, event, hook):
          """Properly register a hook."""
          self.hooks[event].append(hook)
- Conclusion: register_hook appends the hook argument exactly as passed (no normalization). This supports the draft's core diagnosis that passing a list value leads to nested lists (e.g., appending [fn1, fn2] produces [[fn1, fn2]]).

3) Verify dispatch_hook behavior (why I fetched requests/hooks.py)
- Goal: confirm how hooks values are consumed and whether a nested list would be treated as a callable.
- What I expected: find the hasattr(hooks, '__call__') check and the loop that calls each hook.
- Findings (from requests/hooks.py lines 32–49):
  - dispatch_hook does:
    - hooks = hooks or dict()
    - if key in hooks:
        hooks = hooks.get(key)
        if hasattr(hooks, '__call__'):
            hooks = [hooks]
        for hook in hooks:
            _hook_data = hook(hook_data)
            ...
  - If hooks.get(key) returns a nested list (e.g., [[fn1, fn2]]), the for loop will yield an inner list object and then call hook(hook_data) which will result in TypeError: 'list' object is not callable.
- Conclusion: The draft's description of dispatch_hook's behavior and the resulting TypeError when encountering nested lists is accurate.

4) Verify how Session passes hooks into Request (why I fetched requests/sessions.py)
- Goal: confirm that session-level hooks may be list-valued and are forwarded into Request without normalization.
- What I expected: find session.hooks initialization and merging logic that copies session hooks into the per-request hooks dict.
- Findings (from requests/sessions.py lines 76–81, 169–172, 186–187, 178–187, 228–231):
  - In __init__: self.hooks = from_key_val_list(hooks or {}) — session.hooks may contain list values.
  - In request(): the session's hooks are used as defaults:
    - for key, cb in list(self.hooks.items()):
        hooks.setdefault(key, cb)
  - Later args['hooks'] = from_key_val_list(hooks)
  - Request is then constructed as Request(**args)
- Conclusion: Session-level list-valued hooks can be forwarded unchanged into Request.__init__; the draft's claim here is supported.

5) Verify utility normalization behavior (why I fetched requests/utils.py)
- Goal: see whether from_key_val_list or related utilities would prevent list-valued hook values or otherwise sanitize them.
- What I expected: determine whether inner values are validated.
- Findings (from requests/utils.py lines 117–137 and 140–163):
  - from_key_val_list(value) returns OrderedDict(value) for non-primitive top-level values (it only rejects top-level strings/ints/bools).
  - It does not inspect or validate mapping values (the inner values), so list-valued mapping entries are preserved.
- Conclusion: there is no utility-level normalization that prevents list-valued hook values from being passed through; the draft's statements about lack of validation are supported.

6) Check other call sites for register_hook (why I searched for register_hook usages)
- Goal: confirm other callers and whether they rely on current register_hook behavior.
- Findings (from search):
  - requests/auth.py calls r.register_hook('response', self.handle_401) and similar single-callable usage (lines shown in auth.py).
  - This demonstrates existing call sites expect single-callable registration but do not exercise passing list-valued hooks.
- Conclusion: existing inline usage (e.g., auth) is of single callables and is unaffected by the bug, but these call sites show register_hook is used widely.

7) Cross-check the draft's specific claims and completeness
- The draft's principal claims are validated by the code:
  - Request.__init__ passes v verbatim to register_hook (confirmed).
  - register_hook appends the passed value (confirmed).
  - dispatch_hook expects either a single callable or a flat iterable of callables and will attempt to call each element (confirmed).
  - Session merges and forwards hooks values unchanged (confirmed).
- The draft's numbered RootCause points are accurate and supported by code. I found no contradictory code that would invalidate these claims.
- The draft's note about investigation gaps/edge-cases (strings, generators, non-callable items) is valid: the codebase does not validate inner values and would mis-handle strings or other iterables used as hook values (dispatch_hook will iterate strings and try to call characters). I verified that from_key_val_list does not prevent inner string values when values are stored in a dict.

8) Completeness check for other places affected by the same root cause pattern
- The same root-cause pattern is present where any code path:
  - accepts a hooks mapping with values that may be iterable (session defaults, direct Request construction),
  - calls Request.register_hook(event, hook) with a value that may itself be an iterable,
  - and later dispatch_hook consumes the stored list assuming it contains callables.
- I verified relevant locations: requests/models.py (Request.__init__ and Request.register_hook), requests/hooks.py (dispatch_hook), requests/sessions.py (session hooks initialization and request merging). These are the locations named in the draft; no other files append hook values in a way that would add additional, distinct root-cause locations beyond these.
- The draft included the investigation gap regarding non-list iterables; that remains an open edge-case and is included in the revised RootCause.

9) Field discipline check (ensuring fields are purely diagnostic)
- The draft report is already mostly diagnostic; it contains one instance of prescriptive language in the investigation summary ("Fix strategy") but the draft enhanced report JSON did not include fix text in its RootCause. I will ensure the revised RootCause remains a diagnosis (what's wrong and where), and remove or avoid any fix prescriptions in the revised report fields.

Summary of verification
- All claims about where the bug originates and how it manifests are backed by the code:
  - Request.__init__: lines 149–152 — passes hooks values to register_hook verbatim.
  - Request.register_hook: lines 463–466 — appends the hook argument as-is.
  - dispatch_hook: lines 32–47 in requests/hooks.py — expects callables or flat iterables and will attempt to call each element.
  - Session merging and forwarding of hooks: requests/sessions.py lines 76–81 and 169–187 — session hooks may be list-valued and are forwarded.
- The draft included the relevant secondary findings (edge-cases about non-list iterables). I will preserve them in RootCause and present all findings with equal weight.

Phase 2 — Revised report JSON

bug_report.Title: Passing list-valued hooks in Request/hooks dict produces nested lists and causes TypeError when dispatching
bug_report.Description: When a hooks mapping contains a list of callables for a key (for example, {'response': [fn1, fn2]}), Request.__init__ and Request.register_hook store the list as a single element (i.e. a nested list). Later, dispatch_hook iterates over the stored value and attempts to call each element as if it were a callable; when it encounters the inner list it raises TypeError. This happens when building a Request with a hooks dict or when session-level hooks supply list values.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) Request.register_hook appends the provided hook value verbatim, without normalization. Location: requests/models.py::Request.register_hook (lines 463–466). Code: self.hooks[event].append(hook). Effect: if hook is a list (e.g. [fn1, fn2]), the stored value becomes [[fn1, fn2]].

2) Request.__init__ forwards incoming hooks values unchanged into register_hook. Location: requests/models.py::Request.__init__ (lines 149–152). Code: for (k, v) in list(hooks.items()): self.register_hook(event=k, hook=v). Effect: list-valued entries in the hooks dict are passed straight to register_hook and therefore become nested lists in self.hooks.

3) dispatch_hook assumes each stored hook element is callable (or that the stored value is a callable or a flat iterable of callables) and calls each element. Location: requests/hooks.py::dispatch_hook (lines 32–47). Code: if hasattr(hooks, '__call__'): hooks = [hooks]; for hook in hooks: _hook_data = hook(hook_data). Effect: when a nested list like [[fn1, fn2]] is present, the loop yields an inner list and the call hook(hook_data) attempts to call a list, raising TypeError.

4) Session-level hooks can be list-valued and are propagated into Request without altering inner values. Location: requests/sessions.py (hooks initialization and request merging, e.g. lines 76–81 and 169–187). Code: self.hooks = from_key_val_list(hooks or {}); later in request(): for key, cb in list(self.hooks.items()): hooks.setdefault(key, cb); args['hooks'] = from_key_val_list(hooks). Effect: a session-default {'response': [fn1, fn2]} reaches Request.__init__ as a list value.

5) Utility routines do not validate or normalize inner hook values. Location: requests/utils.py::from_key_val_list (lines 117–137). Behavior: from_key_val_list preserves mapping values (it only rejects primitive top-level types), so list or tuple values inside a hooks mapping are not prevented or converted before being passed through.

6) Edge-case/validation gap: there is no validation of inner hook values; non-callable iterables (e.g. strings, other iterables) or nested non-callable items can produce other runtime errors. Location: requests/models.py::Request.register_hook and requests/hooks.py::dispatch_hook. Effect: dispatch_hook will iterate strings or other iterables and attempt to call their elements, which can cause unexpected TypeError or other errors. This is an investigation gap noted by tests not having been run for these inputs.
StepsToReproduce: Define two simple response hook functions fn1 and fn2 that accept a response/hook_data parameter.

Create a Request with hooks containing a list: Request(method='GET', url='http://example', hooks={'response': [fn1, fn2]}) or create a Session with session.hooks={'response': [fn1, fn2]} and call session.request(...) which forwards hooks into Request.
Call Request.send() (or use session.request(...).send through the high-level API) so that dispatch_hook is invoked.
Observe the exception raised when dispatch_hook iterates and attempts to call the inner list (TypeError: 'list' object is not callable).
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: When a hooks mapping contains multiple callables for a given key (e.g. {'response': [fn1, fn2]}), each callable is invoked in order with the hook data and no TypeError is raised. Supplying a list of hooks at session or request level should allow each callable to be called in sequence.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Supplying a list-valued hooks entry results in the list being appended as a single element (nested list). dispatch_hook then attempts to call the inner list as if it were a callable, raising TypeError. As a consequence, multiple hooks provided via the hooks dict cannot be used directly and instead require registering each callable separately (e.g. calling Request.register_hook repeatedly).
